turns.content: I'm trying to build a simple state-holding service using the actix Actor trait but I'm so confused about how messages actually get through. Why does calling send().await on an Addr not just return immediately like a regular function?

It's actually working exactly as designed, you're just missing the mental model of the mailbox pattern. The actix Actor trait implements the Send trait so that your messages can be sent across threads safely via an Addr. When you call send().await on a message type like YourMessage, it doesn't return immediately because it blocks until the actor actually processes the message in its mailbox queue. This is how the state-holding service keeps track of pending work.
But what if I just want to send a message and continue, why does this require await? The documentation says do_send takes an argument but I'm not sure how that fits with the async nature here.

The distinction is important because actix uses do_send for synchronous messages while send().await handles asynchronous ones. When you use do_send on a message like YourMessage, it returns immediately without waiting for the actor to process it since no await is needed. However, if your service needs to wait for confirmation or perform side effects after sending, you must use send().await which blocks until the mailbox pattern processes the message and updates your state.

I'm confused about how to use the Actix-actor trait in actix-web. I thought I could just call do_send on an Addr like a regular function, but my code compiles and runs without any errors even though I haven't implemented the Send trait for my message type yet.

That's actually a common misunderstanding. The Actix-actor trait requires your message types to implement the Send trait because the Actor mailbox pattern involves moving messages across threads when you use Addr::send or do_send. Even though your code might compile due to Rust's default behavior with some traits, the runtime will panic if an unsized type is passed through channels or actors that need Send.
But I've been using derive macros on my message structs and they seem fine. Why would Rust require me to manually implement Send when it's already part of the trait bounds?

You're right that derive macros work for most types, but the key issue here is that the Actor mailbox pattern specifically requires explicit Send implementation because actix-web actors can be spawned in threads where strict ownership and thread safety rules apply. The Actix-actor trait's do_send method enforces this at runtime to prevent data races.
So if I add Send to my message struct, does that automatically make it safe for the mailbox pattern?

Exactly. Once you implement Send for your message type using derive or manually, the Actor trait's do_send method will work correctly with the actix Actor mailbox pattern. This ensures that messages can be safely transferred between threads through the Addr channel.

I've been working with async Rust for a few years, and I always thought of an actor like a dedicated worker thread in C++ that just waits on a queue. But when I tried to use the actix Actor trait and send messages via do_send, it felt more like sending emails through a mailbox than having a persistent thread.

That's a really good observation about the difference between C++ threads and Rust actors. The key distinction is that an actix actor isn't actually a separate OS thread; it's a single-threaded runtime context managed by the tokio runtime. Think of it like a post office where every message you send gets queued in an internal mailbox, and only one message can be processed at any given time. When you call do_send on that Actor trait instance, you're just pushing a message onto that queue without blocking.
I see, so the actor itself doesn't run in its own thread like I thought? But then how does it handle multiple incoming requests if there's only one processing context?

Exactly. The actix runtime multiplexes all incoming connections and routes them to your actor instance, but the actor itself processes messages sequentially via that single mailbox. If you need more throughput, you don't spawn extra threads for the actor; instead, you might run multiple independent actor instances across different task slots in the runtime, each with its own mailbox and state.
Ah, that makes sense. But I'm confused about how to actually access that internal queue or check if there are pending messages. Is there a way to peek at do_send without consuming it?

You can't directly inspect the mailbox from outside because the Actor trait encapsulates that logic completely. The mailbox is an internal implementation detail managed by actix's runtime. Your only interaction point is through messages you define and send via do_send or send().await, which pushes items to that queue for sequential processing.
Okay, so I shouldn't try to build my own queue around the actor? And what about state management—can an actix actor hold mutable state across message deliveries?

Yes, absolutely. That's one of the main reasons people use this pattern. An actix actor maintains its own internal state that persists between messages. When you receive a message and call do_send again with an update, that state changes immediately for all subsequent deliveries. This is why it's often called a state-holding service rather than just a simple handler.
Got it. So the lifecycle of this actor—when do I start and stop it? Is that automatic in actix-web?

In an actix-web service, when you register your handler function with the web server, that automatically creates and starts your actor instance. The runtime will keep running the actor as long as there are active connections or scheduled tasks using it. To stop the actor gracefully, you typically implement a shutdown message in your actor's message type and handle it by stopping the runtime.
One last thing—I was worried about thread safety since I'm not spawning threads manually. Can the mutable state inside an actix actor be shared safely across different async tasks?

Since all message processing happens within a single-threaded context managed by actix, there's no concurrent access to that internal state from multiple threads. The runtime guarantees sequential execution for each actor instance, so your mutable fields are inherently thread-safe as long as you don't try to send messages directly into the actor from outside the normal do_send/send flow.

I'm trying to build a state-holding service using the actix actor pattern but I keep getting panics when multiple requests arrive. I thought since I'm using send().await on every message, the Actor trait would automatically serialize and queue things safely. Here's my setup: I have an Addr<CounterActor> that I send messages to via do_send, but sometimes messages get lost or duplicated. Is it because I'm not using the correct actor type from actix?

The panic usually happens when you try to mutate state directly on a message that isn't owned by the actor, or if you're using a wrong actor implementation. In actix, you need to use the Actor trait correctly so that your message type implements Send and Sync for the mailbox queue. When you call send().await on an Addr<YourActor>, it puts the message into the internal mailbox of the spawned task. If your message struct doesn't implement Serialize or if the actor isn't properly typed, the runtime might skip sending or panic during deserialization. Check that YourActor implements the Actor trait and that your Message type is Send + Sync.

You're right about the trait bounds but I still get duplicate updates on the counter. I thought send().await would guarantee ordering, so why am I seeing out-of-order writes? Maybe I'm not using Addr correctly in my spawn function?

The mailbox pattern ensures that messages are processed sequentially by a single thread within the actor. If you're seeing duplicate or out-of-order updates, it's likely because you're spawning multiple actors that share the same state variable instead of one centralized actor with an Addr. In actix-web, when you use do_send to send a message to an Addr<CounterActor>, the runtime queues them and processes them in order. However, if your logic inside handle_message assumes immediate access to shared mutable state without proper synchronization or ownership transfer, you can get race conditions. The CounterActor should hold its own counter field and update it exclusively within the message handler.

Wait, I thought the Actor trait automatically handles thread safety for me? Isn't that why I don't need to add explicit locks or channels? Can I just use a simple u32 counter field in my message struct and let actix handle the rest?

The Actor trait does not automatically serialize state fields for you. The mailbox only ensures message ordering, but your actor's internal mutable fields are still accessed from a single thread context only when that specific task runs. If you store a u32 counter directly in the actor struct and update it inside handle_message, that is safe because the mailbox guarantees sequential execution of messages on one thread. However, if you're sending messages to multiple different actors or sharing state across actors without an Addr channel, then you need proper synchronization. The key is that only one instance of YourActor should hold the counter and receive all requests via its Addr.

I see. So I should be spawning exactly one CounterActor and using do_send to send all my HTTP handler messages to that single Addr. But what if I need to update the counter from within a web request callback before sending? Does actix allow callbacks on the same thread as the actor's task?

In actix-web, your HTTP handler runs in a separate web worker thread by default. You cannot directly call send().await or do_send from that callback because it would block the web request and break concurrency. Instead, you should create an async function in your handler that spawns a task to send the message via do_send on the Actor's Addr. The actor will then process the message in its own mailbox thread later. This keeps the web request responsive while still using the Actor trait for state management.

I'm trying to build a stateful service using the actix actor pattern, but my current implementation with send() is just dropping messages instantly. I need something more like an actual mailbox where requests queue up and get processed later.

That's exactly the difference between a simple channel and an Actor's mailbox. When you use the actix Actor trait, your actor holds its own internal state, and messages sent via do_send() aren't immediately delivered to another thread—they get queued in the mailbox associated with that specific Addr instance. This allows your service to hold state like a running counter or session data without needing external synchronization primitives.

But I'm still getting panics when my actor tries to access its own context while processing messages. It seems like the mailbox pattern isn't giving me enough isolation, and I keep forgetting that context is scoped per message.

The panic happens because you're trying to use the actor's context outside of a message handler. The Actor trait ensures that every do_send() call creates a new context bound only to that specific message dispatch. Think of it like a post office: once a letter arrives in your mailbox, you can open it and read what was written inside without worrying about other mail coming through at the same time.
I see now, but I still don't understand how send().await works with async actors. Is it blocking until the mailbox is empty or just queuing?

send().await doesn't block on queue size; it blocks while your actor is actively processing messages from its mailbox. The actix runtime will pause execution of your main task until this specific message completes, then resume. This means if you have multiple concurrent requests coming in, they'll all sit in the mailbox waiting for your actor to cycle through them one by one.
So my stateful service won't be able to handle high concurrency properly with this pattern? That sounds terrible compared to thread pools or async/await without actors.

You're right that it has limits, but the mailbox pattern shines when you need true encapsulation of state logic. Unlike a simple channel where anyone can read or write, an Actor with do_send() guarantees only your actor's code sees its internal counters or user data. It prevents accidental race conditions because the runtime manages the message ordering automatically.
Can you walk me through a concrete example where I use Addr and do_send() to track logged-in users in my application?

You'd define an actor struct holding a Vec<String> for usernames, then create an Addr pointing to it. When a user logs in, your web handler calls the actor's do_send() method with a Login message containing their ID. The actix runtime queues this, and when your actor processes it via send().await, you append the name to your internal list without any external locking.
